Generative AI Engineer Roadmap

Generative AI Engineers design AI workflows around large language models, retrieval systems, agents, evaluation sets, guardrails, and product integration. The role is closer to applied software engineering than prompt tinkering.

  1. Home
  2. Careers
  3. Generative AI Engineer

Core skills

  • LLM application design
  • prompt engineering
  • retrieval augmented generation
  • AI evaluation
  • API integration
  • vector search
  • agent workflows
  • guardrails and safety
  • cost and latency optimization
  • OpenAI API

Detailed skill guide

core skills

LLM application design

LLM application design is used in real delivery work, not just theory.

Why it matters: Hiring teams look for evidence that you can apply this skill with quality, judgment, and clear communication.

prompt engineering is used in real delivery work, not just theory.

Tools to practise

  • LangChain
  • LlamaIndex
  • Pinecone
  • PostgreSQL

  • Vercel AI SDK
  • Python

Projects for Generative AI Engineer learners

Project 1: RAG Support Assistant

Build a retrieval assistant that cites documents, refuses unsupported answers, and records evaluation results.

Difficulty: Beginner

Technologies used

  • OpenAI API
  • vector database
  • Python
  • LangChain
  • LlamaIndex

Skills used

  • RAG
  • evaluation
  • source grounding
  • LLM application design
  • prompt engineering

Deployment suggestion: versioned AI service with evaluation gates, observability, fallback behavior, cost controls, and rollback notes. Publish the code with environment setup, sample data or fixtures, and a short operational note.

Learning outcomes

  • Apply RAG to a realistic AI product workflow.
  • Validate quality with golden datasets, retrieval evaluation, prompt regression tests, red-team cases, latency tests, and human review of edge cases.
  • Explain tradeoffs using groundedness, retrieval precision and recall, hallucination rate, latency, token cost, drift, and user correction rate.

Portfolio value: Shows employers you can perform practical Generative AI Engineer work with clear decisions, metrics, and tradeoffs.

Project 2: AI Meeting Brief Generator

Convert notes into structured summaries, action items, risks, and follow-up messages.

Difficulty: Intermediate

Technologies used

  • OpenAI API
  • React
  • FastAPI
  • LangChain
  • LlamaIndex

Skills used

  • structured output
  • prompt design
  • UX
  • prompt engineering
  • retrieval augmented generation

Deployment suggestion: versioned AI service with evaluation gates, observability, fallback behavior, cost controls, and rollback notes. Publish the code with environment setup, sample data or fixtures, and a short operational note.

Learning outcomes

  • Apply structured output to a realistic AI product workflow.
  • Validate quality with golden datasets, retrieval evaluation, prompt regression tests, red-team cases, latency tests, and human review of edge cases.
  • Explain tradeoffs using groundedness, retrieval precision and recall, hallucination rate, latency, token cost, drift, and user correction rate.

Portfolio value: Shows employers you can perform practical Generative AI Engineer work with clear decisions, metrics, and tradeoffs.

Project 3: Prompt Injection Test Harness

Create adversarial tests for an AI assistant and document mitigations.

Difficulty: Advanced

Technologies used

  • LangSmith
  • Python
  • JSON
  • LlamaIndex
  • Pinecone

Skills used

  • security thinking
  • guardrails
  • testing
  • retrieval augmented generation
  • AI evaluation

Deployment suggestion: versioned AI service with evaluation gates, observability, fallback behavior, cost controls, and rollback notes. Publish the code with environment setup, sample data or fixtures, and a short operational note.

Learning outcomes

  • Apply security thinking to a realistic AI product workflow.
  • Validate quality with golden datasets, retrieval evaluation, prompt regression tests, red-team cases, latency tests, and human review of edge cases.
  • Explain tradeoffs using groundedness, retrieval precision and recall, hallucination rate, latency, token cost, drift, and user correction rate.

Portfolio value: Shows employers you can perform practical Generative AI Engineer work with clear decisions, metrics, and tradeoffs.

Learning roadmap

Beginner - 5 weeks

Topics to learn

  • LLM basics: tokens, context windows, temperature, system prompts, and hallucination risk
  • Prompt patterns for extraction, classification, summarization, and structured output
  • API calls, JSON schemas, error handling, and safe logging

Checklist

  • Build evidence for: LLM basics: tokens, context windows, temperature, system prompts, and hallucination risk
  • Build evidence for: Prompt patterns for extraction, classification, summarization, and structured output
  • Build evidence for: API calls, JSON schemas, error handling, and safe logging
  • Learn: LLM basics: tokens, context windows, temperature, system prompts, and hallucination risk, Prompt patterns for extraction, classification, summarization, and structured output, API calls, JSON schemas, error handling, and safe logging
  • Practise with: OpenAI API, Python, Postman
  • Build: Build a document summarizer with structured JSON output and clear failure states.
  • Validate with: golden datasets, retrieval evaluation, prompt regression tests, red-team cases, latency tests, and human review of edge cases
  • Plan release or delivery: versioned AI service with evaluation gates, observability, fallback behavior, cost controls, and rollback notes

Tools

  • OpenAI API
  • Python
  • Postman

Project: Build a document summarizer with structured JSON output and clear failure states.

Outcome: You can call models safely and explain prompt behavior.

Next action: Add evaluation examples before building larger workflows.

Intermediate - 7 weeks

Topics to learn

  • Embeddings, chunking, metadata, retrieval quality, and citation design
  • RAG architecture with source grounding, refusal behavior, and confidence limits
  • Evaluation sets for factuality, relevance, completeness, and tone

Checklist

  • Build evidence for: Embeddings, chunking, metadata, retrieval quality, and citation design
  • Build evidence for: RAG architecture with source grounding, refusal behavior, and confidence limits
  • Build evidence for: Evaluation sets for factuality, relevance, completeness, and tone
  • Learn: Embeddings, chunking, metadata, retrieval quality, and citation design, RAG architecture with source grounding, refusal behavior, and confidence limits, Evaluation sets for factuality, relevance, completeness, and tone
  • Practise with: vector database, LlamaIndex, LangChain, PostgreSQL
  • Build: Build a support assistant that cites policy documents and refuses unsupported answers.
  • Validate with: golden datasets, retrieval evaluation, prompt regression tests, red-team cases, latency tests, and human review of edge cases
  • Plan release or delivery: versioned AI service with evaluation gates, observability, fallback behavior, cost controls, and rollback notes

Tools

  • vector database
  • LlamaIndex
  • LangChain
  • PostgreSQL

Project: Build a support assistant that cites policy documents and refuses unsupported answers.

Outcome: You can build grounded AI features with measurable answer quality.

Next action: Create a test set and measure failures by category.

Advanced - 14 weeks

Topics to learn

  • Tool use, function calling, agent routing, and workflow state
  • Safety filters, prompt injection defense, PII handling, and audit logs
  • Latency, streaming UX, caching, cost controls, and production monitoring
  • Portfolio case studies with architecture, eval results, limitations, and demo videos
  • GenAI system design interviews and product tradeoffs
  • Role-aware CV and interview practice

Checklist

  • Build evidence for: Tool use, function calling, agent routing, and workflow state
  • Build evidence for: Safety filters, prompt injection defense, PII handling, and audit logs
  • Build evidence for: Latency, streaming UX, caching, cost controls, and production monitoring
  • Learn: Tool use, function calling, agent routing, and workflow state, Safety filters, prompt injection defense, PII handling, and audit logs, Latency, streaming UX, caching, cost controls, and production monitoring
  • Practise with: FastAPI, Vercel AI SDK, Redis, LangSmith
  • Build: Ship an AI workflow with tools, traces, cost dashboard, and prompt injection tests.
  • Validate with: golden datasets, retrieval evaluation, prompt regression tests, red-team cases, latency tests, and human review of edge cases
  • Plan release or delivery: versioned AI service with evaluation gates, observability, fallback behavior, cost controls, and rollback notes

Tools

  • FastAPI
  • Vercel AI SDK
  • Redis
  • LangSmith
  • GitHub
  • Loom
  • Career2Day CV Builder

Project: Ship an AI workflow with tools, traces, cost dashboard, and prompt injection tests.

Outcome: You can operate GenAI features like production software. You also have portfolio evidence, CV-ready stories, and a delivery plan for real applications.

Next action: Document risk controls and fallback paths.

Interview preparation

Interview question 1: prompt design

When would you use prompt design in an AI product with retrieval, prompts, model or API calls, evaluation, latency budgets, privacy controls, and user-facing feedback, and what would be the smallest production-like version you would build first? Frame your answer for a first-round technical screen where clarity and fundamentals matter.

Short answer: Explain the role of the concept in a real workflow, name the input and output, and describe the tradeoff rather than giving a dictionary definition. A hiring-quality beginner answer should be concrete: describe the smallest useful implementation, one risk, one test or check, and how to explain the result. For Generative AI Engineer, a good answer should tie prompt design to RAG retrieval quality, validate the work with golden datasets, retrieval evaluation, prompt regression tests, red-team cases, latency tests, and human review of edge cases, watch groundedness, retrieval precision, recall, latency, token cost, drift, false positives, and user correction rate, and explain tradeoffs under protect private data and cite sources where possible. It should mention how the change reaches versioned AI service with evaluation gates, observability, fallback behavior, cost controls, and rollback notes and how risks such as hallucinated answers, private data leakage, weak retrieval, biased outputs, runaway inference cost, and brittle prompt behavior would be reduced.

Detailed answer: Explain the role of the concept in a real workflow, name the input and output, and describe the tradeoff rather than giving a dictionary definition. A hiring-quality beginner answer should be concrete: describe the smallest useful implementation, one risk, one test or check, and how to explain the result. For Generative AI Engineer, a good answer should tie prompt design to RAG retrieval quality, validate the work with golden datasets, retrieval evaluation, prompt regression tests, red-team cases, latency tests, and human review of edge cases, watch groundedness, retrieval precision, recall, latency, token cost, drift, false positives, and user correction rate, and explain tradeoffs under protect private data and cite sources where possible. It should mention how the change reaches versioned AI service with evaluation gates, observability, fallback behavior, cost controls, and rollback notes and how risks such as hallucinated answers, private data leakage, weak retrieval, biased outputs, runaway inference cost, and brittle prompt behavior would be reduced. A top interview response should be specific about the first diagnostic step, the evidence that would confirm or reject the hypothesis, the release or mitigation path, and what would be monitored afterward. It should avoid vague tool-name answers and show judgment about when a simpler design is safer.

Common mistake: Writing long prompts without examples, constraints, or evaluation cases.

Real-world example: Create extraction prompts with schema validation.

Interview question 2: RAG architecture

A junior teammate says RAG architecture works on their machine but fails in review. What exact checks would you ask them to run before changing code? Frame your answer for a live debugging round where the interviewer cares about your investigation order.

Short answer: Reproduce the issue, narrow variables, inspect logs or traces, compare known-good behavior, form a hypothesis, and verify the fix with a regression check. A hiring-quality beginner answer should be concrete: describe the smallest useful implementation, one risk, one test or check, and how to explain the result. For Generative AI Engineer, a good answer should tie RAG architecture to vector database design, validate the work with golden datasets, retrieval evaluation, prompt regression tests, red-team cases, latency tests, and human review of edge cases, watch groundedness, retrieval precision, recall, latency, token cost, drift, false positives, and user correction rate, and explain tradeoffs under measure model quality before changing prompts or models. It should mention how the change reaches versioned AI service with evaluation gates, observability, fallback behavior, cost controls, and rollback notes and how risks such as hallucinated answers, private data leakage, weak retrieval, biased outputs, runaway inference cost, and brittle prompt behavior would be reduced.

Detailed answer: Reproduce the issue, narrow variables, inspect logs or traces, compare known-good behavior, form a hypothesis, and verify the fix with a regression check. A hiring-quality beginner answer should be concrete: describe the smallest useful implementation, one risk, one test or check, and how to explain the result. For Generative AI Engineer, a good answer should tie RAG architecture to vector database design, validate the work with golden datasets, retrieval evaluation, prompt regression tests, red-team cases, latency tests, and human review of edge cases, watch groundedness, retrieval precision, recall, latency, token cost, drift, false positives, and user correction rate, and explain tradeoffs under measure model quality before changing prompts or models. It should mention how the change reaches versioned AI service with evaluation gates, observability, fallback behavior, cost controls, and rollback notes and how risks such as hallucinated answers, private data leakage, weak retrieval, biased outputs, runaway inference cost, and brittle prompt behavior would be reduced. A top interview response should be specific about the first diagnostic step, the evidence that would confirm or reject the hypothesis, the release or mitigation path, and what would be monitored afterward. It should avoid vague tool-name answers and show judgment about when a simpler design is safer.

Common mistake: Indexing documents without checking chunk quality or citation accuracy.

Real-world example: Answer HR policy questions with source citations.

Interview question 3: LLM evaluation

What would you look for in a pull request that introduces LLM evaluation so it is not just a tutorial implementation? Frame your answer for a system design discussion where tradeoffs matter more than tool memorization.

Short answer: Focus on repeatability, reviewability, documentation, tests, secure defaults, and maintainable ownership. A hiring-quality beginner answer should be concrete: describe the smallest useful implementation, one risk, one test or check, and how to explain the result. For Generative AI Engineer, a good answer should tie LLM evaluation to embedding strategy, validate the work with golden datasets, retrieval evaluation, prompt regression tests, red-team cases, latency tests, and human review of edge cases, watch groundedness, retrieval precision, recall, latency, token cost, drift, false positives, and user correction rate, and explain tradeoffs under control cost while keeping acceptable answer quality. It should mention how the change reaches versioned AI service with evaluation gates, observability, fallback behavior, cost controls, and rollback notes and how risks such as hallucinated answers, private data leakage, weak retrieval, biased outputs, runaway inference cost, and brittle prompt behavior would be reduced.

Detailed answer: Focus on repeatability, reviewability, documentation, tests, secure defaults, and maintainable ownership. A hiring-quality beginner answer should be concrete: describe the smallest useful implementation, one risk, one test or check, and how to explain the result. For Generative AI Engineer, a good answer should tie LLM evaluation to embedding strategy, validate the work with golden datasets, retrieval evaluation, prompt regression tests, red-team cases, latency tests, and human review of edge cases, watch groundedness, retrieval precision, recall, latency, token cost, drift, false positives, and user correction rate, and explain tradeoffs under control cost while keeping acceptable answer quality. It should mention how the change reaches versioned AI service with evaluation gates, observability, fallback behavior, cost controls, and rollback notes and how risks such as hallucinated answers, private data leakage, weak retrieval, biased outputs, runaway inference cost, and brittle prompt behavior would be reduced. A top interview response should be specific about the first diagnostic step, the evidence that would confirm or reject the hypothesis, the release or mitigation path, and what would be monitored afterward. It should avoid vague tool-name answers and show judgment about when a simpler design is safer.

Common mistake: Relying on vibes instead of a test set.

Real-world example: Score 100 support answers for groundedness and completeness.

Interview question 4: function calling

FastAPI returns an unexpected result while working on function calling. How would you isolate whether the issue is input data, configuration, code, or environment? Frame your answer for a production incident review where user impact and evidence come first.

Short answer: Separate environment, configuration, data, dependency, and code causes before changing the system. Preserve evidence and avoid broad rewrites. A hiring-quality beginner answer should be concrete: describe the smallest useful implementation, one risk, one test or check, and how to explain the result. For Generative AI Engineer, a good answer should tie function calling to hallucination reduction, validate the work with golden datasets, retrieval evaluation, prompt regression tests, red-team cases, latency tests, and human review of edge cases, watch groundedness, retrieval precision, recall, latency, token cost, drift, false positives, and user correction rate, and explain tradeoffs under protect private data and cite sources where possible. It should mention how the change reaches versioned AI service with evaluation gates, observability, fallback behavior, cost controls, and rollback notes and how risks such as hallucinated answers, private data leakage, weak retrieval, biased outputs, runaway inference cost, and brittle prompt behavior would be reduced.

Detailed answer: Separate environment, configuration, data, dependency, and code causes before changing the system. Preserve evidence and avoid broad rewrites. A hiring-quality beginner answer should be concrete: describe the smallest useful implementation, one risk, one test or check, and how to explain the result. For Generative AI Engineer, a good answer should tie function calling to hallucination reduction, validate the work with golden datasets, retrieval evaluation, prompt regression tests, red-team cases, latency tests, and human review of edge cases, watch groundedness, retrieval precision, recall, latency, token cost, drift, false positives, and user correction rate, and explain tradeoffs under protect private data and cite sources where possible. It should mention how the change reaches versioned AI service with evaluation gates, observability, fallback behavior, cost controls, and rollback notes and how risks such as hallucinated answers, private data leakage, weak retrieval, biased outputs, runaway inference cost, and brittle prompt behavior would be reduced. A top interview response should be specific about the first diagnostic step, the evidence that would confirm or reject the hypothesis, the release or mitigation path, and what would be monitored afterward. It should avoid vague tool-name answers and show judgment about when a simpler design is safer.

Common mistake: Letting the model invent tool outputs instead of validating calls.

Real-world example: Route user requests to calendar, CRM, or search tools.

Interview question 5: prompt injection defense

What security or privacy mistake can happen when using prompt injection defense, and how would you prevent it early? Frame your answer for a senior peer review where maintainability and operational ownership are challenged.

Short answer: Discuss least privilege, input validation, secrets, data exposure, abuse cases, dependency risk, and review controls. Keep examples defensive and safe. A hiring-quality beginner answer should be concrete: describe the smallest useful implementation, one risk, one test or check, and how to explain the result. For Generative AI Engineer, a good answer should tie prompt injection defense to LLM evaluation, validate the work with golden datasets, retrieval evaluation, prompt regression tests, red-team cases, latency tests, and human review of edge cases, watch groundedness, retrieval precision, recall, latency, token cost, drift, false positives, and user correction rate, and explain tradeoffs under measure model quality before changing prompts or models. It should mention how the change reaches versioned AI service with evaluation gates, observability, fallback behavior, cost controls, and rollback notes and how risks such as hallucinated answers, private data leakage, weak retrieval, biased outputs, runaway inference cost, and brittle prompt behavior would be reduced.

Detailed answer: Discuss least privilege, input validation, secrets, data exposure, abuse cases, dependency risk, and review controls. Keep examples defensive and safe. A hiring-quality beginner answer should be concrete: describe the smallest useful implementation, one risk, one test or check, and how to explain the result. For Generative AI Engineer, a good answer should tie prompt injection defense to LLM evaluation, validate the work with golden datasets, retrieval evaluation, prompt regression tests, red-team cases, latency tests, and human review of edge cases, watch groundedness, retrieval precision, recall, latency, token cost, drift, false positives, and user correction rate, and explain tradeoffs under measure model quality before changing prompts or models. It should mention how the change reaches versioned AI service with evaluation gates, observability, fallback behavior, cost controls, and rollback notes and how risks such as hallucinated answers, private data leakage, weak retrieval, biased outputs, runaway inference cost, and brittle prompt behavior would be reduced. A top interview response should be specific about the first diagnostic step, the evidence that would confirm or reject the hypothesis, the release or mitigation path, and what would be monitored afterward. It should avoid vague tool-name answers and show judgment about when a simpler design is safer.

Common mistake: Assuming hidden system prompts cannot be attacked.

Real-world example: Test malicious documents inside a RAG index.

Interview question 6: structured output

What logs, metrics, screenshots, traces, or test output would prove that structured output is behaving correctly? Frame your answer for a hiring-manager conversation where communication and judgment matter.

Short answer: Choose actionable signals, include context for diagnosis, reduce noise, and tie alerts to clear ownership and runbook steps. A hiring-quality beginner answer should be concrete: describe the smallest useful implementation, one risk, one test or check, and how to explain the result. For Generative AI Engineer, a good answer should tie structured output to fine-tuning tradeoffs, validate the work with golden datasets, retrieval evaluation, prompt regression tests, red-team cases, latency tests, and human review of edge cases, watch groundedness, retrieval precision, recall, latency, token cost, drift, false positives, and user correction rate, and explain tradeoffs under control cost while keeping acceptable answer quality. It should mention how the change reaches versioned AI service with evaluation gates, observability, fallback behavior, cost controls, and rollback notes and how risks such as hallucinated answers, private data leakage, weak retrieval, biased outputs, runaway inference cost, and brittle prompt behavior would be reduced.

Detailed answer: Choose actionable signals, include context for diagnosis, reduce noise, and tie alerts to clear ownership and runbook steps. A hiring-quality beginner answer should be concrete: describe the smallest useful implementation, one risk, one test or check, and how to explain the result. For Generative AI Engineer, a good answer should tie structured output to fine-tuning tradeoffs, validate the work with golden datasets, retrieval evaluation, prompt regression tests, red-team cases, latency tests, and human review of edge cases, watch groundedness, retrieval precision, recall, latency, token cost, drift, false positives, and user correction rate, and explain tradeoffs under control cost while keeping acceptable answer quality. It should mention how the change reaches versioned AI service with evaluation gates, observability, fallback behavior, cost controls, and rollback notes and how risks such as hallucinated answers, private data leakage, weak retrieval, biased outputs, runaway inference cost, and brittle prompt behavior would be reduced. A top interview response should be specific about the first diagnostic step, the evidence that would confirm or reject the hypothesis, the release or mitigation path, and what would be monitored afterward. It should avoid vague tool-name answers and show judgment about when a simpler design is safer.

Common mistake: Parsing free-form text when the product needs reliable fields.

Real-world example: Generate task objects from meeting notes.

Interview question 7: multimodal workflows

If you had to deploy a small multimodal workflows project, what setup notes, environment variables, and smoke checks would you include? Frame your answer for a take-home project review where reproducibility and documentation are inspected.

Short answer: Use staged rollout, smoke checks, feature flags or rollback plans where appropriate, and monitor user-impact metrics after release. A hiring-quality beginner answer should be concrete: describe the smallest useful implementation, one risk, one test or check, and how to explain the result. For Generative AI Engineer, a good answer should tie multimodal workflows to GPU utilization, validate the work with golden datasets, retrieval evaluation, prompt regression tests, red-team cases, latency tests, and human review of edge cases, watch groundedness, retrieval precision, recall, latency, token cost, drift, false positives, and user correction rate, and explain tradeoffs under protect private data and cite sources where possible. It should mention how the change reaches versioned AI service with evaluation gates, observability, fallback behavior, cost controls, and rollback notes and how risks such as hallucinated answers, private data leakage, weak retrieval, biased outputs, runaway inference cost, and brittle prompt behavior would be reduced.

A hiring-quality beginner answer should be concrete: describe the smallest useful implementation, one risk, one test or check, and how to explain the result. For Generative AI Engineer, a good answer should tie multimodal workflows to GPU utilization, validate the work with golden datasets, retrieval evaluation, prompt regression tests, red-team cases, latency tests, and human review of edge cases, watch groundedness, retrieval precision, recall, latency, token cost, drift, false positives, and user correction rate, and explain tradeoffs under protect private data and cite sources where possible. It should mention how the change reaches versioned AI service with evaluation gates, observability, fallback behavior, cost controls, and rollback notes and how risks such as hallucinated answers, private data leakage, weak retrieval, biased outputs, runaway inference cost, and brittle prompt behavior would be reduced. A top interview response should be specific about the first diagnostic step, the evidence that would confirm or reject the hypothesis, the release or mitigation path, and what would be monitored afterward. It should avoid vague tool-name answers and show judgment about when a simpler design is safer.

Common mistake: Ignoring image quality, OCR uncertainty, and privacy.

Real-world example: Extract invoice fields from uploaded screenshots.

Interview question 8: latency optimization

What would you measure before trying to optimize latency optimization, and why might an early optimization be harmful? Frame your answer for a scaling discussion where the simplest safe improvement should be justified.

Short answer: Measure first, identify the bottleneck, test one change at a time, and balance latency, throughput, cost, and maintainability. A hiring-quality beginner answer should be concrete: describe the smallest useful implementation, one risk, one test or check, and how to explain the result. For Generative AI Engineer, a good answer should tie latency optimization to inference latency, validate the work with golden datasets, retrieval evaluation, prompt regression tests, red-team cases, latency tests, and human review of edge cases, watch groundedness, retrieval precision, recall, latency, token cost, drift, false positives, and user correction rate, and explain tradeoffs under measure model quality before changing prompts or models. It should mention how the change reaches versioned AI service with evaluation gates, observability, fallback behavior, cost controls, and rollback notes and how risks such as hallucinated answers, private data leakage, weak retrieval, biased outputs, runaway inference cost, and brittle prompt behavior would be reduced.

Detailed answer: Measure first, identify the bottleneck, test one change at a time, and balance latency, throughput, cost, and maintainability. A hiring-quality beginner answer should be concrete: describe the smallest useful implementation, one risk, one test or check, and how to explain the result. For Generative AI Engineer, a good answer should tie latency optimization to inference latency, validate the work with golden datasets, retrieval evaluation, prompt regression tests, red-team cases, latency tests, and human review of edge cases, watch groundedness, retrieval precision, recall, latency, token cost, drift, false positives, and user correction rate, and explain tradeoffs under measure model quality before changing prompts or models. It should mention how the change reaches versioned AI service with evaluation gates, observability, fallback behavior, cost controls, and rollback notes and how risks such as hallucinated answers, private data leakage, weak retrieval, biased outputs, runaway inference cost, and brittle prompt behavior would be reduced. A top interview response should be specific about the first diagnostic step, the evidence that would confirm or reject the hypothesis, the release or mitigation path, and what would be monitored afterward. It should avoid vague tool-name answers and show judgment about when a simpler design is safer.

Common mistake: Making users wait for full responses when streaming or caching would help.

Real-world example: Stream a long answer while retrieving sources.

Interview question 9: AI product UX

Where should AI product UX sit in the flow of an AI product with retrieval, prompts, model or API calls, evaluation, latency budgets, privacy controls, and user-facing feedback, and what should be kept outside that boundary? Frame your answer for a first-round technical screen where clarity and fundamentals matter.

Short answer: Describe components, responsibilities, data or request flow, storage choices, integration points, and what happens when dependencies fail. A hiring-quality beginner answer should be concrete: describe the smallest useful implementation, one risk, one test or check, and how to explain the result. For Generative AI Engineer, a good answer should tie AI product UX to RAG retrieval quality, validate the work with golden datasets, retrieval evaluation, prompt regression tests, red-team cases, latency tests, and human review of edge cases, watch groundedness, retrieval precision, recall, latency, token cost, drift, false positives, and user correction rate, and explain tradeoffs under control cost while keeping acceptable answer quality. Interview question 96: function calling

an AI product with retrieval, prompts, model or API calls, evaluation, latency budgets, privacy controls, and user-facing feedback grows from one team to many teams. Interview question 97: prompt injection defense

Model latency doubles after traffic increases. The first mitigation works, but the root cause is still unclear. How would you investigate whether LLM evaluation, process, or architecture caused it? Frame your answer for a take-home project review where reproducibility and documentation are inspected.

Short answer: Prioritize user impact, stabilize the system, communicate clearly, preserve diagnostic evidence, then run a blameless root-cause review. Quiz questions

Quiz question 1: prompt design

What is the best professional habit when working on prompt design? Consider this during local development. Difficulty focus: beginner.

Answer choices

  • Use the newest tool without defining the problem.
  • Skip validation because the output looks polished.
  • Optimize for demos while ignoring limitations.
  • Use clear instructions, examples, constraints, and test cases.

Correct answer: Use clear instructions, examples, constraints, and test cases.

Explanation: prompt design creates value when it is validated, documented, and connected to a real decision. This is why the scenario matters for Generative AI Engineer work.

Correct answer: Monitor quality, latency, cost, and failures.

A Generative AI Engineer portfolio uses LLM application design. What would make it credible to an interviewer? Consider this before publishing the portfolio project. Difficulty focus: intermediate.

Related careers

  • AI Engineer
  • Machine Learning Engineer
  • AI Research Scientist
  • Data Scientist
  • Data Engineer

Frequently asked questions

How long does it take to become a Generative AI Engineer?

Most learners need 24-34 weeks of focused practice plus portfolio projects that prove applied skill.

What should I build for a Generative AI Engineer portfolio?

Build projects with clear problem framing, reproducible code or process, evaluation, limitations, and a short business or research recommendation.

What is the biggest beginner mistake?

Treating the prompt as the whole product instead of designing retrieval, evaluation, monitoring, UX, and fallback behavior.
